title: Field drainage assessment and maintenance
subtitle: Identifying drainage problems and the low-cost fixes before calling a contractor.
excerpt: A field that holds water in winter is compacted, waterlogged, or draining into a ditch that's blocked. Before investing in pipe drainage or subsoiling, check the ditches, clear the obvious outlets, and assess what's causing the water to sit. Here's how to read a wet field and what to do first.
sourceNotes: SAC Consulting Scotland, Field Drainage on Farms. DEFRA/APHA Agricultural drainage guidance. Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living, land management chapter.

glossaryTerms.definition: A drainage channel created by pulling a torpedo-shaped implement through the soil at 40,60 cm depth, leaving a smooth drainage tube without excavation.
A surface channel cut along the edge of a field to carry away surface water; the receiving end of most field drainage systems.

content.text: Water standing on a field after rain is draining somewhere too slowly, or not at all. The cause is usually one of three things: the soil is compacted and won't absorb rainfall; the subsurface drain pipes are blocked or collapsed; or the
that the drains empty into is silted or blocked. Diagnosing which problem you have before spending money determines whether you need a ditch clear or a full re-drainage.

Walk every ditch on the holding after wet weather. A functioning ditch should be flowing or at least showing recent flow marks. A silted ditch that's half its original depth can't carry its design volume. Overgrown ditch edges and reed growth inside the ditch channel both indicate long-term neglect. Clear blockages and cut vegetation back, this sometimes resolves field drainage without any other work.
Step 2: identify where water is entering

Stand in the wet area and look uphill. Water flows downhill; a wet patch in the middle of a field is usually collecting runoff from higher ground, or it's over a subsurface impermeable layer. Probe the soil with a spade: if the waterlogged layer starts at 30,40 cm and there's a clear compaction layer at that depth, the soil needs aerating before drainage can work properly.
Clear ditch outfalls and cut ditch vegetation, free, annual maintenance task
Open mole drains where the soil type suits it (heavy clay is ideal). Hire a contractor with a mole plough
Slit-aerate compacted areas with a pedestrian aerator; this breaks the surface seal and lets water soak in more easily

If the problem persists after ditch clearing and soil aeration, the subsurface drains are likely failing. A contractor with a drainage survey camera can identify blocked pipe runs and pinpoint where to intervene. Most

last five to ten years in clay soils; they need re-mowing when they've collapsed. If the original pipe drainage is over 30 years old and from clay tile drains, some sections may need full replacement.
